sourceTitle: Get useful insights with workspace and page analytics

questions.question: How do you open the Page Analytics sidebar for a page?

options.text: Click the "🕘 View all updates" icon at the top right of the page, then select the "Analytics" tab
options.explanation: The "View all updates" icon opens a right‑hand sidebar where the Analytics tab resides, giving you access to page analytics.

options.text: Press Ctrl + Shift + A on your keyboard
options.explanation: There is no keyboard shortcut for opening page analytics; it must be accessed via the UI.

options.text: Go to Settings → Workspace → Analytics
options.explanation: That navigation opens Workspace Analytics, not the analytics for an individual page.

options.text: Right‑click the page title and choose "Show analytics"
options.explanation: Right‑clicking the page title does not provide an analytics option.

questions.question: Who is able to view Page Analytics for a given page?

options.text: Anyone who can view the page, even without edit rights
options.explanation: View‑only members cannot see Page Analytics; edit access is required.

options.text: Only the page creator
options.explanation: While the creator can see analytics, any member with edit access can also view them.

options.text: Any workspace member who has editing access to the page
options.explanation: Page Analytics is available to anyone who can edit the page, regardless of whether they created it.

options.text: Only members on the Enterprise plan
options.explanation: Page Analytics is not limited to Enterprise plan members; it works for any user with edit rights.

questions.question: Which type of viewer is NOT listed in the "Viewers" section of Page Analytics?

options.text: Members who have viewed the page while logged in
options.explanation: Logged‑in members are shown in the Viewers list.

options.text: Guests who have access to the page
options.explanation: Guests with access are also displayed as viewers.

options.text: Anonymous viewers who see the page via a public web link
options.explanation: Anonymous, non‑member viewers are excluded from the Viewers list.

options.text: Editors who have made changes to the page
options.explanation: Editors are shown both in the Viewers and the Creators/Editors sections.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Workspace Analytics is correct?

options.text: All workspace members can view the Workspace Analytics dashboard, regardless of plan
options.explanation: Workspace Analytics is only available to organizations on the Enterprise plan.

options.text: Only workspace owners have access to the Members, AI, and Search tabs
options.explanation: Workspace owners can see the Members, AI, and Search tabs; other members see only the Content tab for pages they can edit.

options.text: The Content tab is hidden from members who do not have edit access to any pages
options.explanation: Content analytics are shown to any member who has edit access to at least one page.

options.text: The Overview tab displays only AI‑related metrics
options.explanation: The Overview tab aggregates key data from multiple categories, not just AI.

questions.question: In the Members tab, which metric is most useful for spotting users who may need extra training?

options.text: Number of pages viewed over a selected time period
options.explanation: Low page‑view counts indicate less engagement, signaling that a user might benefit from additional support.

options.text: Date of the user’s most recent activity
options.explanation: While activity date shows recency, it doesn’t directly indicate how much the user is engaging with content.

options.text: Teamspace each member belongs to
options.explanation: Teamspace membership alone doesn’t reveal a member’s activity level.

options.text: Number of AI actions performed by the member
options.explanation: AI actions are tracked in the AI tab, not the Members tab.

questions.question: A knowledge manager wants to prioritize updating popular but stale documents. Which workflow best uses Workspace Analytics to achieve this?

options.text: In the Content tab, sort pages by total views, then examine the last‑edited date column to find popular pages that haven’t been updated recently
options.explanation: Sorting by popularity highlights high‑traffic pages; checking their last‑edited dates reveals which of those are outdated, allowing the manager to prioritize refreshes.

options.text: In the Members tab, filter for users with the most edits and ask them which pages need updating
options.explanation: While power users can provide insight, this approach doesn’t directly identify stale content based on view data.

options.text: Export all workspace data as CSV, then manually count page views in a spreadsheet
options.explanation: Exporting is possible but unnecessary; the Content tab already provides sortable view metrics.

options.text: Check the AI tab for pages most referenced by AI, assuming they are the most important
options.explanation: AI references indicate usage in AI contexts, not necessarily overall popularity or staleness.

questions.question: Which metric in the Search tab most directly signals a content gap in the workspace?

options.text: A high number of searches for a term combined with a low click‑through rate on resulting pages
options.explanation: When many users search for something but few click on results, it suggests the needed content is missing or insufficient.

options.text: Total number of searches performed per day
options.explanation: Overall search volume doesn’t indicate whether specific content is missing.

options.text: Number of unique members performing searches
options.explanation: This metric shows search adoption, not gaps in content.

options.text: Average time spent on search result pages
options.explanation: Time on result pages reflects engagement after a click, not the existence of a needed document.

questions.question: What type of data can you track in the AI tab of Workspace Analytics?

options.text: Active AI members, total AI actions, AI actions per user, and top pages referenced by AI
options.explanation: The AI tab aggregates those specific metrics to show AI adoption and usage patterns.

options.text: Number of page edits made using AI-generated suggestions
options.explanation: AI actions are tracked, but the tab does not break down edits by suggestion source.

options.text: Average response time of AI queries
options.explanation: Response time is not a metric displayed in the AI tab.

options.text: Percentage of pages that have AI-generated summaries
options.explanation: The AI tab does not provide a summary‑generation percentage metric.

questions.question: In what format can you export workspace analytics data for deeper analysis?

options.text: CSV (Comma‑Separated Values)
options.explanation: The article states that analytics data can be exported as a CSV file for further analysis.

options.text: PDF
options.explanation: PDF export is not mentioned as an option for analytics data.

options.text: XLSX (Excel)
options.explanation: Only CSV export is referenced; XLSX is not listed.

options.text: JSON
options.explanation: JSON export is not provided as a built‑in option for analytics.

questions.question: Which workspace analytics tab provides a combined view of key metrics across the other tabs?

options.text: Overview
options.explanation: The Overview tab aggregates a snapshot of key data from Members, Content, AI, and Search.

options.text: Members
options.explanation: Members focuses specifically on individual user activity, not a combined view.

options.text: Content
options.explanation: Content shows engagement metrics for pages only.

options.text: Search
options.explanation: Search displays only search‑related metrics.
